questions.question: Explain the Gaussian distribution assumption in Naïve Bayes for continuous attributes. How are the parameters estimated?
keyPoints: Assumes continuous features follow normal distribution N(μ,σ²) within each class
Parameters μ (mean) and σ² (variance) estimated using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
μ̂ = sample mean, σ̂² = sample variance for each class-feature pair
MLE chooses parameters that maximize likelihood of observing training data

questions.question: Derive the discriminant function for Gaussian Naïve Bayes classifier showing all mathematical steps.
keyPoints: Start with Bayes theorem: P(C|X) ∝ P(X|C) × P(C)
For continuous X, P(X|C) computed using Gaussian PDF
Take log to avoid numerical underflow: log P(C|X) ∝ log P(X|C) + log P(C)
Discriminant: gᵢ(x) = -½∑log(σᵢⱼ²) - ½∑((xⱼ-μᵢⱼ)²/σᵢⱼ²) + log(P(Cᵢ))
Class with maximum gᵢ(x) is predicted

questions.question: Why is the 'naïve' assumption made in Naïve Bayes? Discuss its implications for continuous attribute classification.
keyPoints: Assumes conditional independence: P(X₁,X₂,...,Xₙ|C) = ∏P(Xᵢ|C)
Simplifies estimation - only need mean/variance for each feature-class pair
In reality, features are often correlated but assumption still works well
Works because classification depends on ranking, not exact probability values
Prevents overfitting due to limited parameter estimation from small data

questions.question: Compare Gaussian Naïve Bayes with (i) Multinomial Naïve Bayes and (ii) Bernoulli Naïve Bayes in terms of data types handled.
keyPoints: Gaussian NB: Continuous real-valued features using Gaussian PDF
Multinomial NB: Discrete count data (word frequencies, document term vectors)
Bernoulli NB: Binary/boolean features (presence/absence indicators)
Gaussian uses density, others use probability mass
Different likelihood functions for each type

questions.question: What is the problem of zero variance in Gaussian Naïve Bayes? How can it be handled?
keyPoints: When σ² = 0, the Gaussian PDF has division by zero
Likelihood becomes undefined/infinite
Common solutions: (1) Add smoothing/regularization constant: σ² + ε
(2) Set minimum variance threshold
(3) Use Bayesian estimation with prior on variance
Smoothing prevents overfitting to noise as well

questions.question: A medical diagnostic system uses Gaussian Naïve Bayes with two continuous test results. For disease class: Test1 (μ=50, σ²=25), Test2 (μ=80, σ²=16). For healthy class: Test1 (μ=30, σ²=9), Test2 (μ=40, σ²=25). Priors: P(Disease)=0.3, P(Healthy)=0.7. For a patient with Test1=55, Test2=70, which class is predicted? Show calculations.
keyPoints: Compute likelihoods using Gaussian PDF for each feature
Higher (less negative) is Disease → Predict Disease class

questions.question: Discuss the computational advantages of Gaussian Naïve Bayes in terms of training and testing complexity.
keyPoints: Training: O(n × d) - single pass through data to compute mean and variance for each class-feature pair
No iterative optimization required like gradient descent
Testing: O(d) - just compute d Gaussian PDFs and multiply/add log values
Memory efficient - only store d × C mean/variance pairs
Handles missing data gracefully - simply ignore missing features in calculations
Very fast compared to discriminative models like SVM or neural networks

questions.question: Derive the complete mathematical formulation of Gaussian Naïve Bayes classifier from Bayes theorem. Explain each component and show how classification is performed. Include the discriminant function derivation and discuss how the algorithm handles the continuous attribute case.
keyPoints: Start: Bayes theorem P(C|X) = P(X|C)P(C)/P(X)
For continuous X: P(X|C) cannot be computed directly (infinite values)
Solution: Assume Gaussian distribution, use PDF f(x|μ,σ²)
For multiple features: joint density = product of individual densities (independence)
f(X|Cᵢ) = ∏ⱼ f(xⱼ|μᵢⱼ,σᵢⱼ²) = ∏ⱼ (1/√(2πσᵢⱼ²))e^(-(xⱼ-μᵢⱼ)²/(2σᵢⱼ²))
Take log for numerical stability: log f(X|Cᵢ) = -½∑log(2πσᵢⱼ²) - ½∑((xⱼ-μᵢⱼ)²/σᵢⱼ²)
Discriminant: gᵢ(x) = -½∑log(σᵢⱼ²) - ½∑((xⱼ-μᵢⱼ)²/σᵢⱼ²) + log(P(Cᵢ)) + constant
Classification: Ĉ = argmaxᵢ gᵢ(x)
Explain each term: Mahalanobis distance component, log-prior component

questions.question: Explain the Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) approach for estimating Gaussian Naïve Bayes parameters. Derive the estimators for mean and variance and discuss potential problems when MLE fails (e.g., zero variance cases).
keyPoints: MLE finds parameters that maximize likelihood of observed data
Likelihood: L(μ,σ²) = ∏ᵢ f(xᵢ|μ,σ²) for all samples in a class
Log-likelihood: l(μ,σ²) = -n/2 log(2πσ²) - 1/(2σ²)∑(xᵢ-μ)²
Take derivative w.r.t μ and set to 0: ∂l/∂μ = (1/σ²)∑(xᵢ-μ) = 0 → μ̂ = (1/n)∑xᵢ (sample mean)
Take derivative w.r.t σ² and set to 0: ∂l/∂σ² = -n/(2σ²) + 1/(2σ⁴)∑(xᵢ-μ)² = 0 → σ̂² = (1/n)∑(xᵢ-μ̂)² (sample variance)
Problems: (1) Zero variance when all training points identical - leads to division by zero
(2) Small sample variance causes numerical instability
Solutions: Add smoothing ε (regularization), use Bayesian estimation with priors, set minimum variance threshold
Discuss bias: MLE underestimates variance by factor n/(n-1), but for large n this is negligible

questions.question: Compare Gaussian Naïve Bayes with Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA). Under what conditions do they become equivalent? Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of Gaussian Naïve Bayes as a classification algorithm.
keyPoints: Similarity: Both assume Gaussian distributions and use discriminant functions
Difference: LDA assumes equal covariance matrices for all classes (shared Σ), NB estimates separate Σᵢ for each class
When Σ₁ = Σ₂ = ... = Σₖ, Gaussian NB reduces to LDA

Advantages of Gaussian NB: (1) Simpler/faster, (2) Handles different covariances, (3) Less parameters to estimate, (4) Robust to irrelevant features, (5) Handles missing data, (6) No iterative training

Disadvantages: (1) Conditional independence assumption often violated, (2) Cannot model feature correlations within class, (3) Poor probability estimates when assumption fails severely, (4) Zero variance problem
Discuss when NB fails: when features are highly correlated within classes, when distributions are non-Gaussian
